theory.question: Why are partial failures the defining difficulty of distributed systems, compared to a single machine?
theory.answer:
On one machine, software is usually either fully working or fully broken; in a distributed system, some parts can fail while others keep running, and failures are nondeterministic.

"In a distributed system, there may well be some parts of the system that are broken in an unpredictable way, even though other parts of the system are working fine. This is known as a partial failure."

A single computer with good software tends toward determinism: the same operation produces the same result, and hardware problems usually cause a total crash rather than flaky in-between behavior. Distributed systems cannot hide the messy physical world behind an idealized model. A request involving multiple nodes over a network may sometimes work and sometimes fail unpredictably, and you may not even know whether it succeeded.

That nondeterminism is what makes distributed systems hard, but tolerating partial failures also enables powerful operations like rolling upgrades. The engineering mindset must flip from the happy path to asking what could go wrong, because in a large enough system, rare events happen every day.

Comment: Operations engineers report long-lived partitions, PDU failures, switch failures, whole-datacenter outages, and even physical damage to cooling systems — partial failures are not theoretical.

theory.question: Give examples of network failures when you send a request and get no response.
theory.answer:
When a client sends a request over an asynchronous packet network and hears nothing back, many distinct failures look identical from the sender's side.

"If you send a request to another node and don't receive a response, it is impossible to tell why."

Concrete possibilities: the request was lost (unplugged cable, misconfigured switch); the request is queued and will be delivered later (overloaded network or recipient); the remote node crashed or was powered down; the remote node is paused (e.g., a long GC pause) but will respond later; the node processed the request but the response was lost; or the response is delayed and will arrive later. Timeouts are the usual coping mechanism, but after a timeout you still do not know whether the request was processed — it may still be queued and delivered after you gave up.

Studies and anecdotes make this concrete in production: about 12 network faults per month in one datacenter (half single-machine, half whole-rack disconnects); redundant networking gear does not eliminate human misconfiguration; fiber links cut by animals or scavenging; round-trip times of minutes at high percentiles even within one datacenter; asymmetric partitions where A talks to B and B to C but not A to C; a NIC that drops inbound packets but sends outbound fine; and brief interruptions with long-lasting repercussions.

Comment: Whenever communication crosses a network, it may fail — there is no way around designing for that.

theory.question: Why can't we make network delays predictable on the internet and in typical datacenters?
theory.answer:
Because Ethernet and IP use packet switching with dynamic resource sharing, not circuit switching with reserved bandwidth — delays come from queueing and are therefore unbounded.

"Variable delays in networks are not a law of nature but simply the result of a cost/benefit trade-off."

Circuit-switched networks (classic telephone calls) reserve fixed bandwidth for the duration of a call, so queueing is avoided and maximum end-to-end latency is bounded. Packet-switched networks opportunistically share bandwidth: senders compete for wire capacity, switches decide moment-to-moment which packet goes next, and bursts queue up. That maximizes utilization (cheaper per byte) but introduces variable delay. The same trade-off appears with CPUs: dynamic thread scheduling improves utilization but threads wait unpredictably in the run queue.

Latency guarantees are achievable only with static partitioning — dedicated hardware, exclusive bandwidth — but that is more expensive and reduces utilization. Multitenant clouds optimize for cost, not bounded delay. QoS mechanisms (prioritization, admission control) and newer algorithms like L4S can mitigate queueing, but "currently deployed technology does not allow us to make any guarantees about delays or reliability of the network." Consequently there is no theoretically correct timeout value; you must determine it experimentally.

Comment: InfiniBand and careful QoS can emulate circuit-like behavior in specialized environments, but general-purpose datacenter and internet paths assume unbounded delay.

theory.question: TCP is called 'reliable' — what network problems does it still not solve for application developers?
theory.answer:
TCP handles dropped packets, reordering, and checksum errors within one connection, but applications still face lost connections, unknown processing state, and duplication across reconnects.

"If TCP provides 'reliability,' does that mean we no longer need to worry about networks being unreliable? Unfortunately not."

TCP retransmits when acknowledgments time out, but cannot tell whether the data packet or the ACK was lost. It cannot plug in an unplugged cable and eventually signals an error to the application. Critically, if a connection dies, "you unfortunately have no way of knowing how much data was actually processed by the remote node." A kernel-level ACK means the OS received the bytes, not that the application handled them — you need an application-level success response for certainty. Retransmission and deduplication apply only within one connection; reconnecting can duplicate data.

Sending via a socket also does not mean immediate transmission: data sits in OS buffers until congestion control allows sending, adding queueing delay before packets even enter the network. Congestion control, flow control, and backpressure adapt send rate to capacity, which is good for throughput but contributes to variable latency.

Comment: The same limitations apply in spirit to QUIC, SCTP, and similar transports — convenient message framing, not end-to-end application correctness.

theory.question: Distinguish monotonic clocks from time-of-day (wall-clock) clocks and say which to use for timeouts versus event ordering across nodes.
theory.answer:
Time-of-day clocks return calendar timestamps and can jump backward; monotonic clocks measure elapsed duration and always move forward but are meaningless across machines.

"It is important to distinguish between them, since they serve different purposes."

Time-of-day clocks (e.g., `clock_gettime(CLOCK_REALTIME)`, `System.currentTimeMillis`) return seconds since the Unix epoch and are usually NTP-synchronized so timestamps ideally mean the same across machines. But NTP can forcibly reset a clock backward, leap seconds and DST cause jumps, and resolution was historically coarse. They are unsuitable for measuring elapsed time. Use them for human-facing timestamps and scheduled jobs, not for duration measurement.

Monotonic clocks (e.g., `CLOCK_MONOTONIC`, `System.nanoTime`) are stopwatches: the difference between two readings is a trustworthy duration on one machine. They never jump backward (NTP may slew the rate slightly). The absolute value is arbitrary and cannot be compared across computers. For timeouts and response-time measurement in distributed systems, monotonic clocks on each node are usually fine because they need no cross-node synchronization.

For ordering events across nodes, neither physical clock is safe alone — NTP skew can make a causally later write look earlier (the LWW problem). Logical clocks (version vectors, transaction IDs) are safer for causality.

Comment: Google Spanner's TrueTime returns a confidence interval [earliest, latest] for wall-clock reads rather than pretending nanosecond digits are meaningful.

theory.question: List reasons a process can pause without noticing, and explain why that breaks leader leases and distributed locks.
theory.answer:
A node can be preempted for seconds or minutes at arbitrary points in code; during the pause the rest of the cluster may declare it dead and elect a new leader, yet the paused node resumes thinking it is still leader.

"A node in a distributed system must assume that its execution can be paused for a significant length of time at any point, even in the middle of a function."

Documented pause causes: lock/queue contention (worse on many-core machines); stop-the-world garbage collection; VM suspend during live migration; laptop sleep; hypervisor steal time under heavy load; synchronous disk or network I/O (including surprise classloader reads); swapping/page faults and thrashing; SIGSTOP from an operator. The thread does not know it was paused — it checks the clock later and discovers time jumped.

This breaks lease-based leadership. A leader checks `lease.isValid()` then processes a request, assuming little time passed. But a 15-second GC pause between the check and `process(request)` means the lease expired and another node became leader — yet the old leader still writes, causing split brain and corruption (the HBase lock bug pattern). The problem is like multithreaded race conditions, but worse: there is no shared memory, only messages over an unreliable network, so mutexes do not translate.

Comment: Fencing tokens (monotonically increasing lease numbers checked by storage) protect against zombies after a pause; comparing wall-clock lease expiry to local time adds clock-skew risk on top.

theory.question: How does garbage collection cause pauses, and what can you do to limit its impact on latency-sensitive services?
theory.answer:
GC can stop all application threads for noticeable periods; modern collectors reduce this, but pauses remain a real tail-latency source unless you architect around them.

"Many programming language runtimes (such as the JVM) have a garbage collector that occasionally needs to stop all running threads."

Historically stop-the-world GC could last minutes; modern algorithms (CMS, G1, ZGC, Shenandoah in Java; concurrent mark-sweep in Go) typically pause for milliseconds when tuned. Still, a GC pause at the wrong moment makes a node look dead to peers, triggers false failover, or lets an expired lease holder corrupt data.

Mitigations: tune the collector for your allocation pattern; avoid GC entirely with Rust/Swift-style manual lifetime management; pool and reuse objects to reduce allocation pressure; allocate off-heap; treat GC like a brief planned outage — drain requests from a node before GC, collect with no in-flight work, then resume (hiding pauses from clients and improving tail latencies); or restart processes periodically before long-lived heaps need full GC, one node at a time in a rolling pattern.

None of these fully eliminates pauses in GC'd runtimes, but they reduce impact. For most server-side data systems, hard real-time guarantees are uneconomical, so GC pauses must be designed for, not ignored.

Comment: GC and slow disk I/O can compound — they may stack their delays, which is especially painful on network block devices like Amazon EBS.

theory.question: What is a zombie in distributed locking, and how do fencing tokens protect against zombies and delayed messages?
theory.answer:
A zombie is a former leaseholder that still believes it holds the lease after expiry; without fencing, it can corrupt shared state alongside the new leaseholder.

"The term zombie is sometimes used to describe a former leaseholder that has not yet found out that it lost the lease and is still acting as if it were the current leaseholder."

Two corruption paths: (1) the old client is paused, its lease expires, a new client writes, then the old client resumes and writes too (split brain); (2) the old client crashes after sending a write, the write is delayed in the network for a long time, the lease expires and a new client writes, then the stale write arrives. Shooting the zombie (STONITH — disconnect network, kill VM) is unreliable: it does not help delayed messages, nodes can kill each other, and detection may be too late.

Fencing: the lock service returns a monotonically increasing fencing token with each lease grant. Every write to storage must include the token; storage rejects any write whose token is less than or equal to the highest token already processed. When client 2 gets token 34 and writes, client 1's later write with token 33 is rejected even if client 1 was merely delayed, not malicious. The new leaseholder should write immediately after acquiring the lease to fence off zombies. ZooKeeper's zxid, etcd's revision number, Kafka epoch numbers, and Hazelcast's FencedLock all serve this role. Conditional writes on object storage (S3, GCS, Azure) provide the check.

Comment: Fencing is permanent rejection (unlike optimistic concurrency retry); it requires storage to track the highest seen token or equivalent compare-and-swap semantics.

theory.question: Define limping node, gray failure, and fail-slow; why are they harder to handle than a clean crash?
theory.answer:
A limping node is still alive and may pass health checks but is too degraded to do useful work — also called gray failure or fail-slow — and it is harder to detect and remediate than a node that simply stops.

"Such a situation is called a limping node, gray failure, or fail-slow, and it can be even more difficult to deal with than a cleanly failed node."

Examples: a NIC throttled from 1 Gb/s to 1 Kb/s by a driver bug; a process spending most of its time in GC under memory pressure; worn-out SSDs with erratic latency; hardware affected by heat, vibration, or power issues. Related: a process where one background thread is deadlocked while the rest appears fine. The node responds to pings or lightweight health checks but cannot meet SLOs on real work.

Clean crashes are painful but at least detectable — TCP RST, no heartbeat, timeout. Limping nodes cause timeouts that look like network problems, trigger false suspicion of other nodes, waste resources retrying against a half-working peer, and resist simple failover because the node is not fully dead. Load balancers may keep sending traffic to a node that passes TCP checks but serves 30-second responses.

Comment: Timeouts cannot distinguish slow network from slow node; you need latency SLOs, outlier detection, and circuit breakers — not just alive/dead binary checks.

theory.question: Explain the difference between safety and liveness properties, what type of property is the fencing-token?
theory.answer:
Safety means nothing bad ever happens (and if violated, you can point to the exact moment it broke); liveness means something good eventually happens (may not hold now, but can be satisfied later).

"If a safety property is violated, we can point to the particular point in time that it was broken... After a safety property has been violated, the violation cannot be undone."

For a fencing-token service, uniqueness (no two grants return the same token) and monotonic sequence (if request x finished before y started, then tx < ty) are safety properties — violating them means wrong data was already written. Availability (a non-crashed requester eventually gets a token) is liveness — it might be waiting now, but a response may still arrive. Liveness definitions often include "eventually."

In distributed algorithms, safety is required in all situations the model allows — even if every node crashes, the algorithm must not return a wrong result. Liveness may have caveats: a response is guaranteed only if a majority has not crashed and the network eventually recovers. The partially synchronous model assumes outages last finite duration before repair.

Informal shorthand "nothing bad / something good eventually" is imprecise; the formal distinction matters for proofs and for deciding what your system guarantees under total failure.

Comment: Eventual consistency is a liveness property — replicas may be stale now but should converge eventually.

theory.question: What is L4S (Low Latency, Low Loss, and Scalable Throughput), and what problem does it try to solve?
theory.answer:
L4S is a newer congestion-control and queueing approach that aims to cut latency and loss on packet networks without giving up the high utilization of packet switching.

"New network algorithms like Low Latency, Low Loss, and Scalable Throughput (L4S) attempt to mitigate some of the queuing and congestion control problems both at the client and the router level."

Classic TCP congestion control fills buffers before backing off, so even short queues add delay (bufferbloat). Packet-switched datacenter and internet paths inherently queue under burst traffic, which is why delay is unbounded today. L4S works with explicit congestion signaling so routers can mark congestion earlier and endpoints can react faster, keeping queues short while maintaining throughput. Linux traffic control (TC) also lets applications reprioritize packets for QoS.

In practice today, such QoS and L4S are not broadly enabled in multitenant datacenters and public clouds. Engineers must still assume congestion, queueing, and unbounded delays, and set timeouts experimentally rather than from theory.

Comment: L4S is an evolution path toward lower tail latency on shared networks, not a replacement for application-level timeout, retry, and backpressure design.

theory.question: Contrast crash-stop, crash-recovery, and Byzantine fault models; which model fits most datacenter services?
theory.answer:
Crash-stop nodes die forever; crash-recovery nodes may restart with stable storage; Byzantine nodes may lie arbitrarily. Most datacenter systems assume honest nodes with crash-recovery plus partial synchrony.

"For modeling real systems, the partially synchronous model with crash-recovery faults is generally the most useful."

Crash-stop: a node halts and never returns — simple but pessimistic. Crash-recovery: nodes may crash and later restart; stable disk survives, memory is lost — matches reboots and rolling upgrades. Byzantine: nodes may send malicious or contradictory messages; protocols need supermajorities (>2/3) and are expensive — relevant for aerospace radiation, cryptocurrencies, and peer-to-peer, but not typical internal datacenters where nodes share your organization's software.

Timing models layer on top: synchronous (bounded delay — unrealistic), partially synchronous (mostly bounded, occasionally arbitrary delays — realistic), asynchronous (no timing assumptions at all — very restrictive). Real systems also face limping nodes and amnesia (forgetting disk data), which pure models omit.

Most datacenter designs assume nodes are unreliable but honest: slow, unresponsive, or outdated state, but not deliberately deceptive. Bugs replicated on all nodes are not fixed by Byzantine algorithms — you need diverse implementations for that.

Comment: Weak forms of lying (corrupted packets, bad user input, misconfigured NTP) are handled with checksums, input validation, and NTP outlier rejection, not full Byzantine consensus.

theory.question: How do fault detection, timeouts, and quorum decisions interact when a node stops responding?
theory.answer:
You cannot know a remote node's state without messages, so systems use timeouts to guess failure — but timeouts confuse network delay with node death, and quorums override a node's own belief about whether it is alive.

"If a remote node doesn't respond, there is no way of knowing its state, because problems in the network cannot reliably be distinguished from problems at a node."

Useful signals when available: TCP RST/FIN if the process is gone; explicit error response from the service; ICMP unreachable. Otherwise you wait, retry, and eventually timeout. Variable delay causes false crash suspicion; limping nodes make this worse.

A node cannot trust only itself — it may be asymmetrically partitioned (receives but cannot send), paused for a minute, or declared dead while still running. Distributed algorithms use quorums: if a majority declares a node dead, it must step down even if it feels fine. Majority quorums tolerate minority faults and cannot conflict (only one majority at a time). This is why lease holders must respect quorum eviction and why fencing tokens are needed when they do not.

Comment: The classic "I'm not dead!" scenario — a node can receive traffic but not send replies, or wake from a long pause already declared dead — is why leader election must combine timeouts, quorums, and fencing, not heartbeats alone.

practice.task: [Linux] Test a client-server system's response to network failures with tc/netem and iptables.
practice.description:
Use Linux traffic control (`tc` with `netem`) and firewall rules to inject delays, loss, and partitions without hardware changes — the same class of faults Jepsen nemeses simulate.

Setup — two terminals or VMs on the same LAN (or Docker network). Server on port 8080, client sends HTTP requests in a loop.

```
# Server (Python)
python3 -m http.server 8080

# Client loop
while true; do curl -sf -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code} %{time_total}s\n" http://SERVER:8080/ || echo FAIL; sleep 0.5; done
```

Fault injection commands (run on server or client interface):

```
# 100% packet loss (simulate cable cut to server)
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j DROP

# 50% packet loss + 200ms delay
sudo tc qdisc add dev eth0 root netem loss 50% delay 200ms

# Reorder 25% of packets, delay 500ms
sudo tc qdisc replace dev eth0 root netem delay 500ms reorder 25% 50%

# Partition: block traffic between client IP and server IP
sudo iptables -A INPUT -s CLIENT_IP -j DROP
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -d CLIENT_IP -j DROP

# Cleanup
sudo tc qdisc del dev root; sudo iptables -F
```

Tasks:
1. Measure client p50/p99 latency under clean network vs 200ms netem delay.
2. Under 100% DROP, how long until the client times out? Does it retry? Risk of retry storm?
3. Under partition, does the server log show completed requests the client considers failed?
4. Implement exponential backoff with jitter on the client; compare retry rate under loss before and after.
5. Map each experiment to the indistinguishable failure modes you get with no response: lost request, lost response, delay, partition.

solutions.solution:
Stop the server process to simulate failure.

Killing the server tests crash-stop only. It does not exercise lost responses, asymmetric partitions, delayed packets, or limping behavior. The core lesson is that no-response is ambiguous — process kill makes the cause obvious, so your client never learns to handle timeouts, idempotency, or half-open TCP states.

Use `netem delay 200ms` and observe p99 roughly doubles; `loss 50%` causes timeouts and TCP retransmits (visible in `ss -ti`). 100% iptables DROP leads to client hang until TCP timeout (often 15s–120s depending on sysctl), then `FAIL` — if the client retries immediately, request rate spikes (retry storm risk).

Partition with iptables DROP between client and server: server may complete work for requests sent before the partition while the client timed out — classic "processed but unknown" state. Client must use idempotency keys or verify before retrying writes.

Exponential backoff: `sleep = min(cap, base * 2**attempt) + random jitter` cuts retry rate under sustained loss. Map: DROP ≈ lost request/response; delay ≈ queueing; partition ≈ network partition; partial loss ≈ unreliable network.

Limitation: single-client test does not reveal quorum or leader-election bugs; pair with multi-node tests.

Run a structured fault matrix, monitor server and client sides, and define expected behavior per failure mode.

| Fault | Command | Expected client | Expected server | Failure mode |
|-------|---------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Delay | `netem delay 500ms` | Higher p99, no false crash | Normal processing | Queueing delay |
| Loss | `netem loss 30%` | Retries, TCP retransmit | May see duplicate reads | Lost packet |
| Partition | iptables DROP both ways | Timeout, no leader contact | May still accept if local | Partial failure |
| Asymmetric | DROP OUTPUT only on server | Timeouts | Thinks it replied | Asymmetric partition |
| Limping | `netem delay 5000ms` (no loss) | Very slow, may false-suspect crash | Healthy but useless | Fail-slow / gray failure |

Add server-side request logging with monotonic timestamps and client-side correlation IDs. After partition heals, compare logs: requests with server `200` but client `FAIL` prove ambiguous failure handling is needed.

Client hardening checklist: separate connect vs read timeouts; exponential backoff with jitter; circuit breaker after N failures; idempotent retries for writes; health checks that measure latency SLO not just TCP connect.

Sysctl awareness: `net.ipv4.tcp_retries2`, `tcp_syn_retries` affect timeout duration — document these when reporting results. Cleanup `tc` and iptables after each test.

Trade-off: netem simulates IP-layer faults; it does not inject application pauses (use `kill -STOP`) or clock skew (use `libfaketime`). Combine with Jepsen or DST for full coverage.

practice.task: [Linux] Observe TCP retransmits, bufferbloat, and application-level delays with ss and tcpdump.
practice.description:
TCP hides packet loss via retransmission, but retransmits add unpredictable delay — the gap between transport 'reliability' and application correctness.

Setup: server sends a 10 MB response; client downloads it. Inject loss with netem.

```
# Server
python3 -c "
from http.server import HTTPServer, BaseHTTPRequestHandler
class H(BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):
        self.send_response(200)
        self.end_headers()
        self.wfile.write(b'x' * 10_000_000)
HTTPServer(('0.0.0.0', 8080), H).serve_forever()
"

# Client
curl -o /dev/null -w 'total:%{time_total}s\n' http://SERVER:8080/
```

Inject 5% loss and 100ms delay:

```
sudo tc qdisc add dev eth0 root netem loss 5% delay 100ms
```

Tasks:
1. Run `ss -ti dst SERVER:8080` during download — find `retrans`, `rtt`, `cwnd` fields. How do retransmits correlate with curl total time?
2. Run `tcpdump -i eth0 host SERVER and port 8080 -c 50` — identify duplicate sequence numbers (retransmits).
3. Increase loss to 20%; does throughput collapse due to congestion window shrinkage?
4. Compare `CLOCK_MONOTONIC` elapsed time around `curl` vs wall-clock — any gap indicating client-side pause?
5. Explain: kernel ACK received ≠ application processed the request — a key TCP limitation for distributed systems.

solutions.solution:
Assume TCP reliability means the application can treat a successful `curl` as proof the server processed the request correctly.

TCP ACK confirms the kernel received bytes, not that the application handled them. Retransmits are invisible to the application but inflate `time_total`. Under loss, `ss` shows `retrans:N` — ignoring this hides transport-layer delay that dominates tail latency.

`ss -ti` output includes `rtt:25ms rttvar:5ms cwnd:10 retrans:3/150`. Retransmits increase when netem drops packets; each retrans adds at least one RTT to delivery time, so curl `time_total` jumps disproportionately at higher loss. `tcpdump` shows identical sequence numbers retransmitted — same seq, later timestamp.

At 20% loss, `cwnd` shrinks (congestion control backs off), throughput collapses, and tail latency spikes — bufferbloat plus retransmits compound. This is why "TCP is reliable" does not mean "predictably fast."

Monotonic timing around curl measures pure client wait; wall-clock may differ if NTP adjusts mid-request. Application-level success requires an application response, not a kernel ACK.

Good for understanding transport behavior; does not alone test distributed consensus correctness.

Correlate transport metrics, application latency, and L4S/bufferbloat lessons in one measurement pass.

Procedure:
1. Baseline download — record `time_total`, bytes/sec, `ss retrans` count (should be 0).
2. `netem loss 5% delay 100ms` — record same; expect `retrans > 0`, RTT variance increase, p99 >> p50.
3. `netem loss 20%` — observe cwnd collapse; plot retrans count vs throughput.
4. `tcpdump -nn -c 100 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0 or tcp.len > 0'` — count retransmitted segments (same seq/ack window).
5. Optional: `ss -i` every 100ms during transfer to build retrans/time series.

Interpretation for system design:
- Retransmits are indistinguishable from slow server to the client — both inflate response time.
- Timeouts must be set from measured p99 under fault, not LAN p50.
- Idempotent retries are safe only with server-side deduplication or fencing.
- L4S/explicit congestion marking aims to keep queues short so retransmits are less common — new algorithms mitigate but do not eliminate unpredictability.

Edge case: if the server crashes mid-transfer, TCP may retry until `tcp_retries2` exhausts — client sees long hang then error, with unknown how many bytes the server application processed. Mitigation: Content-Length framing with application-level checkpointing, or chunked responses with explicit commit points.

Cleanup: `sudo tc qdisc del dev eth0 root`. Document `sysctl net.ipv4.tcp_*` values when sharing results — they vary by Linux distribution.

practice.task: [Jepsen] Run fault-injection tests against a small replicated database and interpret the results.
practice.description:
Jepsen is a Clojure framework for testing distributed systems under network partitions, clock skew, process kills, and I/O faults. It has found critical bugs in many production databases.

Prerequisites: Docker Desktop (or Docker + Docker Compose), at least 8 GB RAM, Git.

Setup — clone Jepsen and start its control plane:

```
git clone https://github.com/jepsen-io/jepsen.git
cd jepsen/docker
docker compose up -d
docker compose exec control bash
```

The docker compose stack puts five database hosts on a shared Docker network: `n1`, `n2`, `n3`, `n4`, `n5`. The control container SSHes into them to install and run the database under test — you only need three nodes for a Raft quorum.

Verify SSH from the control container:

```
for n in n1 n2 n3; do ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no $n echo ok; done
```

Deploy a 3-node etcd cluster (recommended) — clone the official Jepsen etcd test; its `db` module installs etcd on `n1`–`n3` and bootstraps the cluster automatically:

```
git clone https://github.com/jepsen-io/etcd.git
cd etcd
lein run test --nodes n1 n2 n3 --time-limit 60 --concurrency 2n --nemesis none
```

Or deploy Redis-Raft on the same nodes (heavier: compiles Redis on each node; allow ~10 min first run):

```
git clone https://github.com/jepsen-io/redis.git
cd redis
lein run test --nodes n1 n2 n3 --time-limit 120 --nemesis none
```

After a run, confirm the etcd cluster (from the control container):

```
etcdctl --endpoints=http://n1:2379,http://n2:2379,http://n3:2379 endpoint status -w table
etcdctl --endpoints=http://n1:2379 member list
```

Example: test the etcd cluster with network partitions and process kills:

```
cd ~/etcd   # or wherever you cloned jepsen-io/etcd
lein run test --nodes n1 n2 n3 --test-count 5 --concurrency 2n --time-limit 60 --nemesis partition,kill --workload register
```

Tasks:
1. Run a baseline test with no nemesis faults and record the history analyzer result (should be `:valid? true`).
2. Enable `:partition-halves` nemesis — half the cluster cannot talk to the other half. Does the client see stale reads, lost writes, or unavailability?
3. Enable `:kill` nemesis on the leader node during writes. After recovery, is the history linearizable?
4. Read Jepsen's `analysis` output: what do `:inconsistent`, `:stale`, and `:lost` mean in the checker?
5. Write one paragraph: which distributed fault class (lost message, pause, partition, delay) does each nemesis simulate?

solutions.solution:
Run the application manually, unplug a network cable, and check if it still works.

This is bad because it is not repeatable, does not explore combinatorial fault timings, and cannot verify correctness properties like linearizability or serializability. A single manual test might miss the race where a leader pause overlaps with a partition — exactly the scenarios Jepsen automates. You also get no history graph or formal analysis, so you cannot tell whether a observed glitch was a safety violation or acceptable liveness delay.

Use Jepsen's Docker control plane, run a test with `:partition-halves` and `:kill` nemeses against a 3-node cluster, and inspect whether `analysis` reports `:valid? false`.

A good run: baseline with no nemesis passes. Under partition-halves, expect either unavailability (if the system refuses writes without a majority) or consistency violations (if both halves accept writes). Under kill on the leader, expect brief unavailability then recovery; the checker tests whether all acknowledged writes are visible after recovery.

Read the Jepsen history: each client operation is a `:invoke` / `:ok` / `:fail` / `:info` entry. The checker (e.g., `linear` for linearizability) builds a dependency graph. If it finds an ordering that violates the consistency model, the test is invalid.

Limitation: you ran one nemesis schedule with fixed timing. Production failures combine GC pauses, delayed packets, and asymmetric partitions in ways a single nemesis type may not cover.

Run a structured Jepsen campaign: baseline, then individual nemeses, then composed faults; map each to distributed fault classes and file actionable bugs.

```
# Composed nemesis example (conceptual lein parameters):
# :nemesis (compose :partition-halves :kill :clock-skew)
# :test-count 20 --time-limit 120 --concurrency 20
```

Procedure:
1. Baseline — confirm checker `:valid? true` and record throughput.
2. `:partition-halves` — maps to network partition: nodes split into components that cannot communicate. Expect split-brain risk if both sides accept writes without quorum.
3. `:kill` / `:pause` (SIGSTOP) — maps to process pause/crash; tests lease expiry, zombie writes, and whether fencing tokens or epoch numbers prevent stale leaders from writing.
4. `:clock-skew` — maps to unreliable time-of-day clocks; tests whether LWW or TTL logic corrupts data.
5. Composed run — partition during leader election; this often finds bugs neither fault alone triggers.

Interpretation: `:lost` means an acknowledged write is unreadable; `:stale` means a read returned an old value when a newer write existed; `:inconsistent` means the checker found an ordering violation. If invalid, capture the minimal history subset Jepsen prints and identify whether it is a safety failure (wrong data returned) or liveness failure (no response within timeout).

Operational notes: Jepsen needs isolated Docker networks; do not run against production. Tests are slow (minutes to hours). False positives happen if timeouts are too aggressive relative to the system's recovery time. File issues with the nemesis schedule, checker used, and the smallest invalid history — that is what Jepsen authors need.

Trade-off: Jepsen tests real binaries (not models) but requires Clojure/JVM expertise and significant setup; it complements but does not replace TLA+ or DST.

practice.task: [DST] Explore deterministic simulation testing with a mocked clock and network.
practice.description:
Deterministic simulation testing (DST) runs your real code with mocked clocks, network, and I/O so failures are reproducible. FoundationDB and TigerBeetle pioneered this; you will build a minimal version.

Setup — create a tiny leader-election simulation in Python:

```
# dst_sim.py
import heapq
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional

@dataclass(order=True)
class Event:
    time: float
    kind: str = field(compare=False)
    node: int = field(compare=False)
    payload: dict = field(compare=False, default_factory=dict)

class SimNetwork:
    def __init__(self):
        self.queue: List[Event] = []
        self.clock = 0.0
        self.dropped = set()  # (src, dst) pairs
        self.delays = {}      # (src, dst) -> seconds

    def send(self, src, dst, kind, payload, delay=0.05):
        if (src, dst) in self.dropped:
            return
        d = self.delays.get((src, dst), delay)
        heapq.heappush(self.queue, Event(self.clock + d, kind, dst, payload))

    def run_until(self, t):
        while self.queue and self.queue[0].time <= t:
            ev = heapq.heappop(self.queue)
            self.clock = ev.time
            yield ev

class Node:
    def __init__(self, id, sim):
        self.id = id
        self.sim = sim
        self.is_leader = False
        self.lease_until = 0.0
        self.paused_until = 0.0

    def on_heartbeat(self, payload):
        if self.sim.clock < self.paused_until:
            return  # GC pause — ignore messages
        if payload.get('leader') == self.id:
            self.is_leader = True
            self.lease_until = self.sim.clock + 2.0
```

Tasks:
1. Schedule a 5-second GC pause on the leader at t=1.0; inject an election at t=2.0. Does the paused node think it is still leader after resume?
2. Drop all packets from node 0 to node 1 from t=3 to t=8 (partition). Log whether two leaders exist simultaneously.
3. Replay the exact same event schedule twice; confirm identical final state (determinism).
4. Add a fencing token counter; reject writes with stale tokens after re-election.
5. Compare DST to Jepsen: what does DST give you that fault injection does not?

solutions.solution:
Run unit tests with `time.sleep()` to simulate delay.

`sleep` uses wall-clock time, is slow, non-deterministic under load, and cannot explore thousands of interleavings. You cannot replay the exact failure. DST requires controlling all nondeterminism sources — clocks, network, thread scheduling — through a simulator.

The heap-based simulator delivers events in deterministic order. When node 0 is paused (`paused_until = 6.0`) during an election at t=2, it misses heartbeats and node 1 becomes leader — but node 0 still has `is_leader = True` and `lease_until` in the past after resume unless you clear state on pause expiry. That reproduces the zombie leader bug.

Partition by adding `(0, 1)` and `(1, 0)` to `dropped` — both sides may elect themselves if each has a local majority view. Logging `is_leader` per node at each event shows split brain windows.

Replaying the same seed and event schedule yields identical state — the core DST advantage. Adding `fencing_token` incremented on each election and rejecting `write` when `token < max_seen` fixes the safety violation.

DST explores more schedules faster than manual tests; limitation: your Python sim is not the production runtime — real DST (FoundationDB Flow, TigerBeetle VOPR, Antithesis) patches or replaces nondeterministic primitives in actual code.

Build a seeded DST harness that runs hundreds of random fault schedules and shrinks failing traces.

```
import random

def run_scenario(seed):
    rng = random.Random(seed)
    sim = SimNetwork()
    nodes = [Node(i, sim) for i in range(3)]
    # Random: pause leader 0-10s, partition random pairs, delay 0.01-2s
    pause_at = rng.uniform(0, 5)
    pause_dur = rng.uniform(1, 8)
    nodes[0].paused_until = pause_at + pause_dur
    # ... drive elections, writes, check invariants
    return check_no_split_brain(nodes) and check_fencing(sim)

for seed in range(1000):
    assert run_scenario(seed), f"failed seed {seed}"
```

Invariants (safety): at most one leader with a valid lease; `max_fencing_token` monotonic; no write accepted with stale token. Liveness (optional, with fairness): eventually one leader if partition heals.

On failure, replay seed `seed` — DST gives you a minimal reproducible trace. Jepsen cannot guarantee replay because real wall-clock fault injection does not control scheduling order.

Three DST integration strategies used in production: application-level (single event loop — TigerBeetle), runtime-level (sequential goroutine scheduler — MadSim/FrostDB), machine-level (deterministic hypervisor — Antithesis). Choose based on how much code you can restructure.

Edge cases to include: pause longer than lease; message delivered after partition heals with old epoch; simultaneous elections. Trade-off: DST requires discipline (no `HashMap` iteration order bugs, no uncontrolled threads); pay that cost for databases where correctness bugs are catastrophic.

practice.task: [TLA+] Model-check a distributed lock with lease expiry and a zombie writer.
practice.description:
TLA+ lets you specify a protocol and use the TLC model checker to explore states and verify invariants. You will model a classic distributed lock bug: a client pauses, its lease expires, another client acquires the lease, and the first client resumes and writes.

Install:

```
# macOS
brew install tlaplus

# Or download TLA+ Toolbox from https://github.com/tlaplus/tlaplus/releases
```

Create `Lock.tla`:

```
---- MODULE Lock ----
EXTENDS Naturals, Sequences

VARIABLES leaseHolder, leaseExpiry, fencingToken, storageValue, maxTokenSeen

Init ==
  /\ leaseHolder = 0
  /\ leaseExpiry = 0
  /\ fencingToken = 0
  /\ storageValue = ""
  /\ maxTokenSeen = 0

GrantLease(client) ==
  /\ leaseHolder = 0
  /\ leaseHolder' = client
  /\ fencingToken' = fencingToken + 1
  /\ leaseExpiry' = leaseExpiry + 3
  /\ UNCHANGED <<storageValue, maxTokenSeen>>

\* Client paused past expiry — lease times out
ExpireLease ==
  /\ leaseHolder # 0
  /\ leaseExpiry' = 0
  /\ leaseHolder' = 0
  /\ UNCHANGED <<fencingToken, storageValue, maxTokenSeen>>

\* Write WITHOUT fencing check (buggy)
WriteUnsafe(client, val) ==
  /\ leaseHolder = client
  /\ storageValue' = val
  /\ UNCHANGED <<leaseHolder, leaseExpiry, fencingToken, maxTokenSeen>>

\* Write WITH fencing token (fixed)
WriteSafe(client, val, token) ==
  /\ leaseHolder = client
  /\ token = fencingToken
  /\ token > maxTokenSeen
  /\ storageValue' = val
  /\ maxTokenSeen' = token
  /\ UNCHANGED <<leaseHolder, leaseExpiry, fencingToken>>

Next ==
  \/ \E c \in {1,2} : GrantLease(c)
  \/ ExpireLease
  \/ \E c \in {1,2}, v \in {"A", "B"} : WriteUnsafe(c, v)

Spec == Init /\ [][Next]_<<leaseHolder, leaseExpiry, fencingToken, storageValue, maxTokenSeen>>

\* Safety: only the current leaseholder's write should persist after a re-grant
NoCorruption ==
  leaseHolder = 0 => TRUE  \* simplified; refine for your model
====
```

Tasks:
1. Run TLC on the unsafe version with `CONSTANTS` and small state bound. Find the state where client 1 writes after client 2 holds the lease.
2. Add `WriteSafe` and an invariant `FencingOK == maxTokenSeen = fencingToken` (or that stale tokens are rejected). Verify TLC passes.
3. Write the safety property in one sentence: what must never happen?
4. Write the liveness property: what should eventually happen?

solutions.solution:
Read the lock code and reason informally that a pause could cause problems.

Without model checking you miss interleavings: grant → pause → expire → re-grant → zombie write can require a specific ordering that code review overlooks. You also cannot distinguish a safety violation (corruption already happened) from a liveness issue (lease eventually granted). The whole point of TLA+ is exhaustive exploration of small state spaces that humans do not enumerate.

TLC on the unsafe model finds a counterexample trace: `GrantLease(1) → ExpireLease → GrantLease(2) → WriteUnsafe(1, "A")` while client 2 holds the lease — if your model allows zombie writes when `leaseHolder` was not cleared on client 1's side. The fix is to require fencing tokens on storage writes.

Safety property: "storage must never accept a write from a client whose fencing token is less than or equal to the highest token already processed." This is a safety property — once violated, corruption is permanent.

Liveness property: "every client that requests a lease eventually receives one if the lock service does not crash." TLC may need fairness constraints (`WF_` or `SF_`) to prove liveness; without fairness, a liveness property can fail vacuously.

Good models catch the bug class; limitation: the model may omit network delay (stale messages arriving after re-grant), which requires a separate `WriteDelayed` action.

Model both the zombie-pause path and the delayed-message path; verify safety with fencing and document liveness caveats.

Extend the spec with:

```
VARIABLES delayedWrites  \* queue of <<client, val, token>>

SendDelayedWrite(c, v, t) ==
  /\ delayedWrites' = Append(delayedWrites, <<c, v, t>>)
  /\ UNCHANGED <<leaseHolder, leaseExpiry, fencingToken, storageValue, maxTokenSeen>>

DeliverDelayedWrite ==
  /\ delayedWrites # <<>>
  /\ LET w == Head(delayedWrites)
     IN /\ IF w.token > maxTokenSeen
           THEN /\ storageValue' = w.val
                /\ maxTokenSeen' = w.token
           ELSE UNCHANGED <<storageValue, maxTokenSeen>>
        /\ delayedWrites' = Tail(delayedWrites)
     /\ UNCHANGED <<leaseHolder, leaseExpiry, fencingToken>>
```

Invariants to check:
- `NoStaleWrite`: `maxTokenSeen` monotonically increases and no `storageValue` change uses `token <= maxTokenSeen`
- `LeaseExclusive`: at most one `leaseHolder # 0` at a time

TLC counterexample on unsafe model: client 1 gets token 1, lease expires, client 2 gets token 2 and writes, delayed write from client 1 with token 1 arrives — without fencing, corruption; with fencing, rejected.

Safety vs liveness mapping: uniqueness and monotonicity of fencing tokens are safety (violation pinpointed at the duplicate/stale write step); lease availability is liveness ("eventually granted" — only if lock service and network eventually work).

Operational notes: keep state bounds small (`MAX_TOKEN = 3`, 2 clients) or TLC explodes. Model is not the implementation — add comments linking spec actions to real code paths and re-verify when the protocol changes. For production systems, CockroachDB and Kafka maintain TLA+ specs alongside code.

Trade-off: model checking proves the algorithm, not the Java/Go implementation; combine with Jepsen or DST on the real binary.
